coredata.dc:title: A new model for predicting thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils using the soil-water characteristic curve
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Journal of Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Hao
Vanapalli, Sai K.
coredata.dc:description: Heat and mass transfer processes in porous media, such as soils, strongly depend on thermal conductivity. In contrast to homogeneous materials, the thermal conductivity of soils, especially when they are unsaturated, is highly complex due to the intricate interactions among solid, water, and air phases. Water saturation is one of the most important factors influencing the thermal conductivity. Current models for predicting thermal conductivity, whether empirical, based on mixing theories, or grounded in percolation theory frequently exhibit limitations under varied environmental conditions. To address these challenges, in this study a new model is developed for predicting the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils, utilizing the Soil-Water Characteristic Curve (SWCC) as a fundamental tool. The proposed approach explicitly links pore-scale thermal conductivity to pore size distribution, subsequently upscaling this relationship to predict normalized thermal conductivity at the macroscale. The model incorporates two parameters, n 1 and η, both of which are strongly related to the pore size distribution. The parameter n 1 is derived from the SWCC while an empirical correlation is suggested between n 1 and η, facilitating practical implementation. The model’s accuracy is validated against a wide range of experimental datasets, demonstrating reliable prediction performance across various soil types and temperature conditions. This model can be effectively used in thermo-hydro-mechanical (THM) coupled modeling for unsaturated soils.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermal conductivity
Unsaturated soils
SWCC
van Genuchten model

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relationship between the SWCC and thermal conductivity for unsaturated soils
A new thermal conductivity model for unsaturated soils
Comparison with other models in literature

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Soil Water Characteristic curve showing distinct zones of desaturation (modified after Vanapalli et al. [18]).
Temperature dependence of surface tension and density of water [32,33].
Changes in the ratio of water surface tension to density with temperatures.
Normalized thermal conductivity as a function of water saturation for granite soils: (a) porosity = 0.26; (b) porosity = 0.32; (c) porosity = 0.36; (d) porosity = 0.53.
Normalized thermal conductivity as a function of water saturation for different types of soils: (a) Soil #5; (b) Soil #7; (c) Soil #9; (d) Soil #11.
Normalized thermal conductivity as a function of water saturation for Canadian soils: (a) Soil #26; (b) soil # 29; (c) Soil #30; (d) Soil #23.
Temperature dependence of thermal conductivity: (a) Soil #20; (b) Soil #21.
Measured and estimated SWCCs for selected soils, (a) Soil #16; (b) Soil #17; (c) Soil #18; (d) Soil #19.
Measured and predicted normalized thermal conductivity calculated by SWCCs(a) Soil #16; (b) Soil #17; (c) Soil #18; (d) Soil #19.
Comparisons between the measured and calculated normalized thermal conductivity of 30 soils samples.
Model comparison for different soils: (a) Soil #12; (b) Soil #13; (c) Soil #14; (d) Soil #15.
Relationship between normalized thermal conductivity and water saturation when varying the parameter n 1.
Relationship between normalized thermal conductivity when varying the parameter η.
Relationship between the parameter n 1 and the parameter η.
Soil physical properties and parameters used in the model validation.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: AB is the abbreviation for Alberta; BC is for British Columbia; MB is for Manitoba; NB is for New Brunswick; NS is for Nova Scotia; ON is for Ontario; PE is for Prince Edward Island; QC is for Quebec; and SK is for Saskatchewan

head.ce:title: A new model for predicting thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils using the soil-water characteristic curve

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, University of Ottawa, Ottawa, ON, K1N 6N5, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil Engineering, University of Ottawa, Ottawa, ON, K1N 6N5, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Civil Engineering, University of Ottawa, Ottawa, ON, K1N 6N5, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heat and mass transfer processes in porous media, such as soils, strongly depend on thermal conductivity. In contrast to homogeneous materials, the thermal conductivity of soils, especially when they are unsaturated, is highly complex due to the intricate interactions among solid, water, and air phases. Water saturation is one of the most important factors influencing the thermal conductivity. Current models for predicting thermal conductivity, whether empirical, based on mixing theories, or grounded in percolation theory frequently exhibit limitations under varied environmental conditions. To address these challenges, in this study a new model is developed for predicting the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils, utilizing the Soil-Water Characteristic Curve (SWCC) as a fundamental tool. The proposed approach explicitly links pore-scale thermal conductivity to pore size distribution, subsequently upscaling this relationship to predict normalized thermal conductivity at the macroscale. The model incorporates two parameters, n 1 and η, both of which are strongly related to the pore size distribution. The parameter n 1 is derived from the SWCC while an empirical correlation is suggested between n 1 and η, facilitating practical implementation. The model’s accuracy is validated against a wide range of experimental datasets, demonstrating reliable prediction performance across various soil types and temperature conditions. This model can be effectively used in thermo-hydro-mechanical (THM) coupled modeling for unsaturated soils.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heat and mass transfer in porous media is of fundamental importance due to its relevance across diverse disciplines, including mechanical, chemical, and geotechnical engineering. Applications range from heat exchangers and ceramics to nuclear waste disposal and CO₂ sequestration [1–4]. Accurate modeling of these processes requires the consideration of coupled physical phenomena, including the interactions between heat, moisture, and mechanical stress. To capture these complexities, multiphysics simulation frameworks such as thermal-hydraulic (TH), thermo-hydro-mechanical (THM), and thermo-hydro-mechanical-chemical (THMC) models are employed for reliable representation of the complex interactions occurring within porous media [5,6]. A key parameter in these models is the thermal conductivity (λ) of the porous medium. Unlike homogeneous materials, the thermal conductivity of unsaturated porous media depends on the spatial distribution and interactions among the solid, liquid, and gas phases. Among these, water saturation exerts the most significant influence. A wide variety of models, including empirical relationships, mixing theories, and percolation-based approaches, have been proposed to estimate thermal conductivity in unsaturated materials [7,8]. However, these models often suffer from limited generality, reliance on difficult-to-measure parameters, or challenges in integration with existing simulation frameworks. As these models have been comprehensively reviewed by Dong et al. [7], a detailed summary is omitted here.

In recent years, the Soil Water Characteristic Curve (SWCC) has become a widely accepted tool for modeling hydraulic and mechanical behavior of unsaturated soils [9,10]. A similar approach has also shown promise for predicting thermal conductivity, owing to the SWCC’s reflection of the pore structure and water retention behavior. The SWCC describes a physical relationship between water content and matric suction, which is closely related to the pore size distribution that governs thermal conduction pathways. Several studies have explored the functional similarity between the SWCC and thermal conductivity curves [11–13]. However, many of these models adopt SWCC-like functional forms by analogy, without providing a rigorous theoretical foundation.

For example, Likos [11] proposed an empirical model for thermal conductivity based on the Brooks–Corey SWCC equation [14]:
(1)
λ
( ψ )
=
{
λ dry
+
(
λ crit
−
λ dry
)
(
ψ crit
ψ
)
β ′
ψ crit
< ψ
λ crit
+
(
λ sat
−
λ crit
)
(
ψ b
ψ
)
β
ψ b
< ψ ≤
ψ crit
λ sat
ψ ≤
ψ b
where λ sat, λ dry represent the thermal conductivities of saturated and dry soils, respectively; λ crit denotes the critical thermal conductivity marking the transition near the pendular-function boundary; ψ cirt is corresponding suction in SWCC; ψb, β, and βʹ are fitting parameters.

Similarly, Lu & Dong. [13] proposed a thermal conductivity model based on the van Genuchten SWCC equation [9]:
(2)
λ −
λ dry
λ sat
−
λ dry
= 1 −
[
1 +
(
θ
θ f
)
m T
]
1 /
m T
− 1
where θf and mT are fitting parameters.
The model was evaluated using 25 datasets covering both coarse grained (i.e., sands) and fine-grained (i.e., clayey soils) demonstrating satisfactory agreement with observed values.

The SWCC is widely used in thermo-hydro-mechanical (THM) simulations to characterize key soil behaviors such as shear strength [10], hydraulic conductivity [9], and mass–volume relationships [15]. If thermal conductivity could also be predicted from the SWCC, it would enable a unified framework for coupled THM modeling of unsaturated soils. However, both models mentioned above are derived by analogy with traditional SWCC functions and do not rest on a solid theoretical foundation. Furthermore, the parameters used to describe SWCC and thermal conductivity are not inherently linked, which limits the predictive value of the SWCC in estimating thermal conductivity.

To address these limitations, this study proposes a closed-form analytical model for predicting the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils. The model is developed based on the fundamental behavior described by the SWCC and uses two parameters, one of which can be directly obtained from the SWCC. This approach improves both the theoretical clarity and the practical usability of the model. An empirical relationship between the two parameters is also established based on data from 30 soil samples, which enhances the model’s applicability and ease of implementation. The performance of the proposed model is evaluated using a broad dataset covering a range of soil types and temperature conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relationship between the SWCC and thermal conductivity for unsaturated soils
ce:section.ce:para: During wetting and drying cycles, the water saturation in soils changes as the volume of air within the pore space varies. Since soils are composed of solid, liquid, and gas phases, shifts in the relative proportions of these phases significantly affect key properties such as hydraulic conductivity and thermal conductivity [16]. For example, during drying, water that normally fills soil pores is gradually replaced by air. Because water has a relatively high thermal conductivity and air has a much lower one, this replacement reduces the soil’s ability to conduct heat [17]. As a result, the thermal conductivity of the soil decreases as water saturation decreases. Therefore, thermal conductivity is strongly influenced by the distribution and relative proportion of the pore phases, particularly water and air. When changes in pore structure are neglected, the thermal conductivity of a given soil in its unfrozen state can be considered a function of water saturation. This relationship is closely tied to the Soil Water Characteristic Curve (SWCC), which describes how water content varies with suction. An example is shown in Fig. 1 .

The SWCC has been used as a tool to predict and interpret the hydraulic and mechanical properties of unsaturated soils [19]. This is primarily because the SWCC captures the redistribution of water and air within the pore structure. Building on this concept, the influence of water saturation on thermal conductivity can also be described using the SWCC framework. In this study, a modified form of the van Genuchten equation is adopted to estimate thermal conductivity, due to its proven effectiveness in fitting the SWCC for a wide range of soil types.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A new thermal conductivity model for unsaturated soils

ce:section.ce:para:
As discussed in Section 2.1, the thermal conductivity of soils depends on the phase transitions within the soil system. For the drying process, the boundary conditions for thermal conductivity are
λ sat
and
λ dry
, respectively. In our research, we use a normalized thermal conductivity function, λn, to describe the variation in thermal conductivity:
(3)
λ =
λ dry
+
λ n
·
(
λ sat
−
λ dry
)

The next step is to determine the expression for the normalized thermal conductivity function, λ n. To do so, we first examine the phase transition in soils. During drying, the pressure point at which air first enters the largest soil pores can be determined by the pressure difference between air and water, described by the Young-Laplace equation [19]. Consequently, the phase transition function λ n should depend on pore size. At the beginning of drying, no phase transition occurs, λ n = 1, all the pores are filled with water, r = R max. As drying progresses, water is excluded from soil pores and air enters, causing λ n to decrease with decreasing pore size. Eventually, when all the pores are filled with air and the phase transition is complete, λ n = 0.

As the next step, we introduce the concept of pore-scale thermal conductivity, λp . Based on the assumption that soil pores can be represented as a collection of spherical pores, Gemant and Likos [12,20] proposed different approaches for modeling pore-scale thermal conductivity. Likos [12] calculated the pore-scale thermal conductivity, λp , using mixing models and then upscaled it to estimate the thermal conductivity of the soil:
(4)
{
1
λ p
=
x f
1
λ
sat
+
( 1 −
x f
)
1
λ
dry
Series
λ p
=
x f
λ
sat
+
( 1 −
x f
)
λ
dry
Parallel
λ p
=
λ
sat
x f
λ
dry
( 1 −
x f
)
Geometric
where xf is defined as the fraction area of liquid in a unit cell, which depends on the filling angle. In other words, the pore-scale thermal conductivity depends on the phase changes within a unit pore. Eq. (4) is the basis for using pore scale thermal conductivity to derive the thermal conductivity of soils. However, since Eq. (4) includes three different models for λp , it remains unclear which is most suitable for practical applications. Furthermore, calculating xf requires knowledge of the filling angle, a parameter that is not readily available from conventional soil measurements. As a result, deriving a closed-form analytical expression for soil thermal conductivity directly from Eq. (4) is highly challenging.

To simplify the analysis, we consider a horizontal cylindrical capillary tube with radius R, subjected to heat flow in the x-direction. The variable r denotes the radial position within the pore, ranging from 0 to R. It is assumed that heat transfer occurs via conduction through solid particles, water, and air, while convective and radiative mechanisms are neglected. Under these assumptions, the relationship between pore-scale thermal conductivity and the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils can be obtained by integrating over the pore-size distribution function f(r):
(5)
λ =
∫
R min
R max
λ p
f
( r )
dr +
λ
dry

From a theoretical standpoint of view, integrating Eq. (5) requires a functional relationship for λp . Assuming the pore-scale thermal conductivity is proportional to the fraction area of the phase change element, the following relationship can be derived:
(6)
λ p
∝ π
r
2
Eq. (6) describes heat flow as being represented by a group of capillary tubes, each with a uniform pore size, r.

Inspired by Mualem [21], who conceptualized water flow as capillary tubes with variable radii, we extended this approach to characterize heat flow in unsaturated soils. This extension involves introducing the probability, ae (r, ρ) of the connection of two different pores (r and ρ) and effective radius re :
(7)
λ p
∝ π
r e 2
a e
(
r , ρ
)
d r d ρ

The effective radius for the capillary tube can be approximated by:
(8)
r e
=
r ρ

The probability ae is given by:
(9)
a e
(
r , ρ
)
= f
( r )
f
( ρ )
d r d ρ

Inserting Eqs. (8) and (9) into Eq. (7), and using Eq. (5) we obtain:
(10)
λ ∝
∫
∫
r f
( r )
ρ f
( ρ )
d r d ρ
=
[
∫
R min
R max
r f
( r )
d r
]
2

Eq. (10) is not an explicit function. Therefore, we propose using the normalized thermal conductivity, Eq. (3), which is the ratio of thermal conductivity, to eliminate the constant values in Eq. (10). This approach facilitates effective water saturation to define the boundary condition, when r → R max, Se → 1, and r → R min, Se → 0. The normalized thermal conductivity can be calculated by:
(11)
λ n
=
λ −
λ dry
λ sat
−
λ dry
=
[
∫
0
S e
r f
( r )
d r
∫

1
r f
( r )
d r
]
2

From the Young-Laplace equation [19], the radius of the capillary tube is related to the capillary pressure, Pc , which is given by:
(12)
r =
2 γ cos
θ c
P c
where γ is surface tension at the air-water interface; θc is the contact angle.

Besides, the pore size can also be expressed by water saturation [22]:
(13)
d
S e
= f
( r )
d r

Substituting Eq. (12) and Eq. (13) to Eq. (11) gives:
(14)
λ n
=
[

P c

1
d
S e

]
2

There are various models of Se (pc ) in the literature. Among them, the widely used model is van Genuchten function [9]:
(15)
S e
(
P c
)
=
[
1
1 +
(
α
P c
)
n
]
m
where α, m, and n are the van Genuchten parameters related to the soils; and m is defined by m = 1- 1/n.

Troch [23] proposed a relationship by setting m = 1 + 1/n 1 to replace m = 1 - 1/n, the van Genuchten model yields a closer fit to experimental data for a variety of soil types. The corresponding function is shown below:
(16)
S e
(
P c
)
=
[
1
1 + α
(
P c
)
n 1
]
(
1 + 1 /
n 1
)

Eq. (16) has been validated and applied in the SWCC; and relative hydraulic conductivity against published experimental data covering a wide range of soils textures from sand to clay [24,25]. In our study, the modified van Genuchten model (Eq. (16)) is used in the following calculation. Integrating Eq. (15), substituting Eq. (14), and replacing the value of 2 in Eq. (14) with a power η to extend it to a more general case, yields (Appendix A):
(17)
λ n
=
[
1 −
(
1 −
S e
1 / m
)
m
]
η
where m = 1 + 1/n 1, with m and η being fitting parameters related to the pore size distribution of soils, and with m > 1.

n 1 can be obtained by fitting the modified SWCC van Genuchten model to experimental data. η comes from the function of pore-scale thermal conductivity which is related to pore size. Eq. (17) is applicable for estimating the thermal conductivity of soils where heat transfer occurs primarily through conduction via soil components. This is because the equation is derived under the assumption that heat transfer takes place through contact among soil particles, water, and air, while convective and radiative mechanisms are neglected.

As discussed in earlier sections, one of the primary motivations for investigating the thermal conductivity of unsaturated porous media is its importance in simulations involving thermal–hydraulic–mechanical (THM) coupling, where environmental temperature acts as a key input parameter. Accordingly, it is crucial to determine whether the thermal conductivity of porous media remains constant or varies with temperature. If thermal conductivity is temperature-dependent, the governing equations must be modified to incorporate this effect. Experimental studies have demonstrated that the thermal conductivity of soils is indeed influenced by environmental temperature [26,27], highlighting the need to extend existing models to account for this dependency.

Numerous SWCC models have considered the influence of temperature [28,29]. The general approach involves adjusting the surface tension and contact angle based on temperature changes, followed by deriving equations for the capillary water and adsorbed water components of the SWCC separately. Vahedifard et al. [28] developed various non-isothermal SWCC models based on traditional SWCC formulations (e.g., van Genuchten and Brooks-Corey models) [30]. However, their models are highly complex and require additional fitting parameters. In contrast, Gao et al. [31] investigated the temperature effect on soil hydraulic conductivity and simplified the temperature influence on SWCC by focusing on changes in the water-air surface tension and the temperature dependence of SWCC is determined using the relationship below:
(18)
{
S
e , T
= δ
S
e ,
T 0
δ =
(
γ T
ρ T
/
γ
T 0
ρ
T 0
)
n 1
+ 1
where δ is the ratio represents the temperature effect on surface tension, γ is the surface tension; ρ is density of water.

To account for environmental temperature variations, we will incorporate the influence of temperature into our equation. Given that our model is based on the SWCC equation, we propose modifying the SWCC equation to include temperature effects, thereby deriving a new thermal conductivity function. Consequently, Eq. (17) is formulated to incorporate temperature effects directly into the thermal conductivity calculations (details are provided in Appendix B):
(19)
λ
n , T
=
[
1 −
(
1 −
(
δ ·
S
e ,
T 0
)
1 / m
)
m
]
η

Eq. (19) presents the normalized thermal conductivity function, incorporating temperature dependence, and it will be analyzed in detail in the following section. From Eq. (19), it is evident that the surface tension ratio is a function of temperature. In the work of Gao et al. [31], the surface tension ratio was not derived from experimental data but was instead based on an assumed relationship. In this study, data from Gittens [32], who measured how the surface tension of water changes with temperature, was used. Furthermore, the temperature dependence of liquid water density has been extensively studied. For convenience, we utilize the correlation function proposed by Marcolli et al [33]., which is valid for temperatures ranging from −223 °C to 120 °C and is given as follows:
(20)
ρ
( T )
= 1864.3535 − 72.5821489 T + 2.5194368
T
2
− 4.9000203 ×
10
− 2
T
3
+ 5.8602531 ×
10
− 4
T
4
− 4.5055151 ×
10
− 6
T
5
+ 2.2616353 ×
10
− 8
T
6
− 7.3484974 ×
10
− 11
T
7
+ 1.4862784 ×
10
− 13
T
8
− 1.6984748 ×
10

T
9
+ 8.3699379 ×
10
− 20
T
10
where
ρ ( T )
is density of liquid water in units of kg/m3.

The temperature effect on surface tension and water density, δ, can be calculated using Eq. (18). The resulting ratio is then plotted in Fig. 2 and Fig. 3
.

To examine the performance of the proposed models (i.e., Eq. (17), Eq. (19)) for the normalized thermal conductivity function, a database of the measured thermal conductivity from 26 soil samples is collected from the literature [34–38]. These soils represent various soil from weathered granite soils, pure sand, to clayey soils and are used evaluate our proposed models by fitting each equation to experimental measurements and discuss models’ parameters. The physical properties of the selected soils are summarized in Table 1 . In this study, the root-mean-square error (RMSE) was used to evaluate the performance of the proposed models:
(21)
R M S E =
∑
j = 1
X
(
λ j p
−
λ j m
)
2
X
where X is the total number of measured data points.
λ j p
and
λ j m
are the jt
h predicted and measured thermal conductivity values.

Fig. 4 shows the experimental data and fitting results of normalized thermal conductivity for granite soils with varying porosity. The results indicate that porosity strongly influences thermal conductivity. For soils with lower porosity, normalized thermal conductivity increases linearly with Se , while soils with higher porosity exhibit a nonlinear relationship. The experimental and calculated results align well, highlighting trends driven by interactions between thermal conductivity and water saturation. Initially, thermal conductivity depends on interparticle contact points, with air dominating the pore space. As water films form, both gas and fluid phases coexist, leading to a rapid increase in conductivity. At higher saturation, the gas phase becomes discontinuous, causing nonlinearity. For lower-porosity soils, the solid matrix dominates, making this nonlinearity less pronounced. Conversely, higher porosity amplifies the nonlinear effect.

The Lu database includes the water saturation dependence of the normalized thermal conductivity in two silty clay loam, sand, sandy loam, loam, silty loam and clay loam soils [38]. For saving space, four experimental results and calculation results are only plotted, as shown in Fig. 5 .

The RMSE value indicates an excellent match between calculated and the measured results. The results suggest the new model can describe the normalized thermal conductivity across a wide range of soil types. For sands, the normalized thermal conductivity curves show dramatically increases trends at lower water saturations, while for loamy and clayey soils this trend is relatively slow in response to water saturation increases. This large difference can be explained by the surface area of different types of soils. For loamy and clayey soils which have a considerable large surface area, more water is needed to form the continuous heat flow path. According to the discussion in section, there should be a threshold water saturation that separated the water saturation into gas phase continuous and both gas and fluid phase continuous stage. For sand soils which have a relatively large quartz fraction, this threshold water saturation is not obvious, for loam and silt loam in this study, this threshold water saturation is about 0.2 as shown in Fig. 5.

To evaluate the proposed model, we used 9 samples from the Tarnawski et al. [34]. databases, containing 40 soil samples from 9 Canadian provinces. In this database, the thermal conductivity was measured at 6 different water saturation levels (i.e., 0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.7, and 1.0). The soil texture includes sand, loam, silt loam, silty clay. For saving space, only four samples are preset here, as shown in Fig. 6 .

The RMSE values for the summarized Canadian soils data indicates excellent match between theory and the measurements results. The new model was able to describe the normalized thermal conductivity across a wide range of soil types.

To evaluate the performance of Eq. (19), we used a database from Nikolaev, which includes temperature-dependent thermal conductivity data for two soils: Ottawa sand and Richmond Hill fine sandy loam [36]. According to the equation, an initial thermal conductivity curve at a specific temperature is required to predict thermal conductivity at various environmental temperatures. We first fitted the thermal conductivity data for the two soil samples at 2 °C and then used the equation to calculate thermal conductivity for these soils at different temperatures. The calculated results are presented in Fig. 7 .

As shown in Fig. 7, the predicted results agree well with the measured data. Specifically, our model captures the trend of increasing soil thermal conductivity with rising environmental temperatures. The common explanation for this phenomenon is the influence of temperature on latent heat and vapor migration, particularly at high temperatures where vapor migration significantly impacts heat transfer. Our model explains this behavior through its foundation on the SWCC, allowing us to relate the thermal conductivity increase to changes in matric suction.

At the same water saturation, Se , higher temperatures reduce matric suction due to decreased surface tension and changes in the energy state of water. This reduction, observed in several experiments, leads to thicker water films and more continuous liquid bridges within the soil, enhancing thermal pathways and increasing thermal conductivity. However, our model overestimates thermal conductivity at high water saturation. This discrepancy likely arises because our model cannot accurately estimate matric suction at high water saturation. Without SWCC data to refine the equation parameters, the parameters obtained from fitting the thermal conductivity data at 2 °C fail to fully capture the SWCC trend. Specifically, the absence of the air entry value in our analysis may contribute to this overestimation.

It is also important to note that, because our model is based on SWCC, it primarily considers the influence of matric suction. At higher environmental temperatures, such as 70 °C, where vapor migration dominates the heat transfer process, our model is not applicable

The first step in calculating the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils is fitting the SWCC data to obtain the parameter n 1 in the modified van Genuchten model (Eq. (16)). Dong et al. [39] and Duc Cao et al. [37] measured both the SWCC and water saturation dependence of thermal conductivity data. The selected samples include sand, silt, clayey and kaolinite. By fitting the SWCC data using Eq. (16), n 1 can be obtained and subsequently used to calculate the normalized thermal conductivity of these samples (Eq. (17)). The fitting results of the SWCCs, shown in Fig. 8 , demonstrate strong performance across a wide range of soil textures. The obtained fitting parameters of modified van Genuchten model are summarized in Table 2 .

Fig. 9 shows the results of the measured thermal conductivity against the fitted results. Additionally, Fig. 10 displays the comparison of the calculated and measured values of normalized thermal conductivity for all samples in the database, demonstrating good agreement across all the data used in this study. The optimized parameter in Eq. (17) and RMSE value for each of the soil samples are presented in Table 1. The lower RMSE value indicates an excellent match between fitting and the experiments results. The parameter n 1 is obtained from SWCC data, the optimized parameter η obtained by fitting thermal conductivity data for Accusand, Bonney, Hopi clayey sand, and Kaolinite, is 0.11, 3.25, 1.32, and 1.59. In theory, the two fitting parameters are related to pore size distribution as they are derived based on the van Genuchten 1980) model. Thus, there should be a relationship between these two parameters. The parameter analysis is summarized in the next section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison with other models in literature
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, our model is compared with the SWCC-based model proposed by Lu & Dong. [13] (Eq. (2)) and an empirical model proposed by Lu et al. [38] both of which have been widely used for predicting the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils. The thermal conductivity function proposed by Lu et al. [38] is expressed as follows:
(22)
λ
n
= exp
{
α T
[ 1 −
S e
(
α T
− 1.33 )
]
}
where αT is a soil texture dependent parameter.

Fig. 11 illustrates the comparison between the proposed model Eq. (17) and the two models from literature across varying water saturations. As shown, the predictions from Eq. (17) exhibit excellent agreement with the measured data, consistently aligning with observed values across the entire range of saturation levels. The Lu et al. [38] model performs well, even though it includes only one parameter; however, it tends to slightly overestimate thermal conductivity at lower saturations and underestimate at mid-range saturations. In contrast, the Lu & Dong [13] model consistently underestimates thermal conductivity at higher saturations. This may be because its mathematical form causes the normalized conductivity to approach unity too slowly, resulting in predicting lower values near full saturation. This comparative analysis underscores the improved accuracy and reliability of Eq. (17), highlighting its suitability for effectively capturing the relationship between thermal conductivity and water saturation in soils.

Fig. 12 illustrates the relationship between normalized thermal conductivity and water saturation for different values of parameter n 1. As n 1 increases from 0.3 to 2.0, the steepness of the curve changes, indicating that smaller values of n 1 (e.g., 0.3) result in a more gradual increase in normalized thermal conductivity with saturation. Conversely, higher values of n 1 (e.g., 2.0) lead to a more shaper rise, showing that the system becomes more sensitive to small changes in saturation near full saturation.

This parameter primarily controls how quickly the system responds to increasing water saturation. Lower values of n 1 suggest a more sensitive response in the early stages of saturation, while higher values delay the response until higher saturation is reached, which can be linked to the soil’s ability to retain water in its pores.

Fig. 13 presents the influence of η on normalized thermal conductivity across varying water saturation levels. As η increases from 1 to 10, the curve becomes progressively flatter, particularly at lower saturation levels, resulting in a more sigmoidal shape. Smaller values of η (e.g., 1) cause the thermal conductivity to increase rapidly, while higher values of η delay the increase. The parameter η influences the gradualness of the curve, where higher values suggest a more gradual, delayed response of thermal conductivity to changes in water saturation. This reflects a more uniform distribution of water in the system and indicates the material’s capacity to handle water movement across a broader saturation range.

For reasonable modeling the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils, reliable determination of both the proposed parameters
n 1
. and η is required. These parameters, which govern the shape and sensitivity of the thermal conductivity curve, are inherently linked to the soil’s pore size distribution. The parameter n 1 is typically derived from the SWCC data, making it relatively accessible in practice (as discussed in Section 5.1). However, obtaining η directly from experimental data is more complex, posing a challenge for applying the model broadly in practice. In our study, based on fitting 30 soil samples, typical parameter ranges observed for
n 1
and η are:
n 1
: ranges from 0.49 to 5.21
η : ranges from 0.06 to 7.73

These parameter ranges cover a variety of soil textures including sand, loam, silt loam, and clay loam. The diversity in these values demonstrates the model's broad applicability and flexibility across different soil conditions.

Given the relationship between these parameters and the underlying pore structure of soils, it is logical to hypothesize that a direct empirical correlation exists between n 1 and η. To explore this, we conducted a comprehensive analysis of 30 soil samples from the literature, covering a wide range of soil types and conditions. Through fitting the thermal conductivity data from these diverse samples, we derived an empirical equation that links
n 1
and η , as shown in Fig. 14 .
(23)
n 1
= 1.39 /
(
η + 0.212
)

This empirical equation reveals a strong inverse relationship between the two parameters. As η increases, n 1 decreases, reflecting a fundamental connection between pore structure and thermal behavior. Soils with a higher η, which indicates a more gradual change in thermal conductivity with increasing water saturation, tend to have more uniform pore distributions. Conversely, soils with a lower n 1 exhibit a sharper response, characteristic of less uniform pore structures where small changes in water saturation led to more significant variations in thermal conductivity.

The empirical relationship was determined based on the best-fitting data from these diverse soil samples, demonstrating a robust fit across the dataset. However, it is important to acknowledge that this equation represents just one possible relationship between these parameters and is specifically valid for the soil samples analyzed in this study.

While the current findings are promising, further research and data collection are required to refine and generalize this relationship for broader applications. Additional studies involving a wider variety of soil types and conditions will be necessary to confirm the robustness of this empirical equation and to explore potential variations in its form. As such, this relationship should be viewed as a preliminary step towards a more comprehensive future understanding of the interaction between n 1 and η , with the potential for further refinement as more data becomes available.

The thermal conductivity model proposed in this study uses the widely used Soil-Water Characteristic Curve (SWCC) to represent moisture-dependent heat transfer by upscaling the pore scale thermal conductivity. Since SWCC is already fundamental in geotechnical engineering for estimating shear strength, hydraulic conductivity, and volume change behavior, its extension to thermal conductivity allows for a unified framework in thermal–hydraulic–mechanical (THM) simulations.

Using two parameters, one of which can be directly obtained from SWCC by fitting the model, enables efficient estimation of thermal conductivity across a wide range of moisture conditions without the need for specialized thermal testing. The strong inverse correlation observed between two parameters, n 1 and η, simplifies parameter estimation. This feature makes the model highly adaptable for use in various geotechnical applications where unsaturated conditions and temperature variability are significant. These include energy foundation systems, ground-source heat pumps (GSHPs), borehole thermal energy storage (BTES), and unsaturated soil covers used in landfills and environmental barriers. In such systems, accurately representing moisture-dependent thermal conductivity is critical for performance evaluation, system design, and long-term thermal management.

However, the model’s current formulation is limited to steady-state conduction-dominated heat transfer. In high-temperature conditions, where vapor diffusion or convective effects may dominate, the model’s assumptions may no longer hold. Additionally, the empirical relationship between n 1 and η requires further validation across a wider range of soil textures and mineralogical compositions to confirm its general applicability.

Future research should aim to evaluate the model’s performance under transient thermal conditions, including high-temperature applications where vapor migration contributes significantly to heat transfer. Applying the model within fully coupled THM simulations and validating its performance using field-scale data will further support its integration into advanced numerical tools. Such development will enhance the model’s role in sustainable geotechnical engineering, particularly in energy-efficient infrastructure and climate-adaptive design.

This study presents a new model for predicting the thermal conductivity of unsaturated soils by utilizing the SWCC. The model introduces two parameters, n 1 and η, which are both related to the soil’s pore size distribution. Notably, n 1 can be obtained directly from the SWCC data. To enhance the model’s practical applicability, an empirical relationship between n 1 and η was established using best-fit results from 30 soil samples. This relationship enables the estimation of η solely from n 1, thereby simplifying the predicting thermal conductivity.

Hao Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Sai K. Vanapalli: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The first authors would like to acknowledge the funding received from the China Scholarship Council and University of Ottawa, Canada for the Ph.D. research program. The second author would like to acknowledge the funding support received as 2020 Discovery Grant (5808) from NSERC, Canada for this project.

ce:section.ce:para: The derivation of the normalized thermal conductivity function is similar with van Genuchten hydraulic conductivity function, which can be found in many unsaturated soils textbooks [22].

Replacing the power 2 with η in Eq. (14), and we have:
(A1)
λ n
=
[
∫
0
S e
d
S e
P c
∫
0
1
d
S e
P c
]
η

Combining Eq. (A1) with Eq. (15) gives:
(A2)
λ n
=
[
F (
S e
)
F ( 1 )
]
η
where
(A3)
F
(
S e
)
=
∫
0
S e
[
(
x ′
)
1 / m
1 −
(
x ′
)
1 / m
]
1 / n
d
x ′

Substitution of
x ′
=
(
y ′
)
m
into Eq. (A3) yields:
(A4)
F
(
S e
)
= m
∫
0
S e
1 / m
(
y ′
)
m − 1 + 1 / n
[
1 − (
y ′
)
]
− 1 / n
d
y ′

In the van Genuchten model, m = 1–1/n, a closed-form expression can be derived:
(A5)
λ n
=
[
1 −
( 1 −
S e
1 / m
)
m
]
η
Replacing m = 1 – 1/n with m = 1 + 1/n 1, one arrives at Eq. (17).

The detailed derivation of Eq. (19) is presented below. The expression for the van Genuchten model is given as:
(B1)
S e
=
1
[
1 +
(
α
P c
)
n 1
]
m
where Pc is capillary pressure, Se is effective water saturation; α, m, and n 1 are fitting parameters, and m = 1 + 1/n 1.

Using Eq. (B1), the water saturation at temperature T can be expressed as a function of the water saturation at a reference temperature
(B2)
S
e , T
=
[
1 +
(
α
P
c ,
T 0
)
n 1
]
1 + 1 /
n 1
[
1 +
(
α
P
c , T
)
n 1
]
1 + 1 /
n 1
×
S
e ,
T 0

Eq. (B2) can be approximately replaced by the following expression
(B3)
S
e , T
=
(
α
P
c ,
T 0
)
1 +
n 1
(
α
P
c , T
)
1 +
n 1
×
S
e ,
T 0

Assuming the capillary pressure at temperature T can be expressed as a product of the temperature effect ratio δ and the capillary pressure at a reference temperature, we have:
(B4)
P
c , T
= δ
P
c ,
T 0

Combining Eq. (B3) and Eq. (B4)
(B5)
S
e , T
=
δ
n 1
+ 1
S
e ,
T 0
Substituting Eq. (B5) into Eq. (17), we arrive at Eq. (19).
